INFORMS SF Bay Area · March 2026

How to Prototype AI Products
That Actually Drive Revenue

Building AI features is easy. Building AI features that drive real business outcomes is not.

Speaker
Anastasiia Moskovchenko
Product Lead @ RepRally · ex-Phillips, Yandex · Startup mentor @ Techstars NY
Goal today
3–5 insights you can use
to build AI prototypes that ship
About RepRally

B2B wholesale marketplace.
Brands → Reps → Stores.

Operating system for physical retail distribution. Brands pay commission on every sale. Independent reps execute in the physical world. Stores get trending products with low minimums.

500K+ stores TAM70+ marketsGreylock-backedAI recommendations

Every order flowed through a rep. Classic answer: design → build → test = 3+ months. My answer: prototype in one week.

The Market Has Already Shifted

6 months ago: 7 people.
Today: 3 people. Same output.

⬅ 18 months ago — 7 roles
Product Designer AI replaced
QA Engineer AI replaced
Frontend Engineer AI replaced
Backend Engineer AI replaced
Product Analyst AI replaced
Product Manager AI replaced
CTO / Tech Lead
✓ Today at RepRally — 3 roles
CTO still here
Full Stack Engineer still here
Product (me) still here

The future belongs to horizontal, entrepreneurial roles — people who think about how to build the product, not just execute within their lane.

The Gemini PM just changed his title to Member of Technical Staff. Because now everyone on the team can build.

How My Process Changed With AI

Before: every prototype needed
a full team & weeks.

Old process: I had an idea → write a PRD → wait for designer → wait for engineer → wait for QA → test → discover it's wrong → repeat.

❌ Before AI — typical timeline
PRD writing2–3 days
Designer mockup1 week
Engineer prototype2 weeks
QA + iterate1 week
Total: 4–6 weeks to learn if idea works
✓ Now with AI — same result
Describe to Claude + Stitch2 hours
Load real data into prompt30 min
Test with 3 users1 day
Iterate → hypothesis2 days
Total: 3–4 days. Engineers validate, not build from scratch.
The Toolkit — No Engineers Required

A modern PM is part analyst,
part designer, part engineer.

🤖

Claude

Writes prototype code, analyzes data, iterates on logic. Zero engineer time on hypothesis validation.

Code genData analysis
✦

Stitch by Google

AI-native canvas. Text → UI → interactive prototype → Figma/React export. Layout variations in seconds.

UI genPrototypesDesign systems
📊

Real Data

The most important tool. Actual store coordinates, order history, density — fed into prompts from day one.

The truth layer

A beautiful Stitch prototype without real data is still a pretty lie. The tool changes. The principle doesn't.

Building AI features
is easy.
Building AI features
that work is not.

What that process revealed about unit economics, system constraints, and the gap between "AI-generated output" and actual value.

Engineer lines in prototype

Key metric in 3 months
1 PM
Sole product manager
Insight 1 — You Don't Need to Be a Designer

Design affects 80% of purchase decisions.
Here's how to diagnose it.

The user "hires" your product to do a job. Bad design makes that job harder — and leads to guaranteed losses. You don't need a designer's eye. You need a framework.

👁

Step 1 — Diagnose

Take a screenshot. Ask Claude: "What are the 3 biggest UX problems here? What psychological principles are violated?"

🔬

Step 2 — Ask users

Don't ask what they want. Ask about the last time they used it. What was hard? What did they skip? Facts, not opinions.

🧪

Step 3 — Hypothesis

"If we [change X], then [metric] will improve for [user segment]." Now test it.

❌ Weak hypothesis

"If we add a background to the push widget, CTR will grow for old users."

No segment clarity. No causal logic. Cosmetic fix.

✓ Strong hypothesis

"If we show rarely-active users their best unseen content (not latest), Smiles Rate and watch depth will grow for that cohort — and retention follows."

Insight 2 — Psychological Principles That Move Metrics

How you say it matters
as much as what you say.

🖼 Framing Effect

The same fact, framed differently, drives different behavior.

❌ "80% churn rate"✓ "20% retention"
❌ "Book now"✓ "Check availability"
"Reframe this CTA to feel less committing and more exploratory."

😰 Loss Aversion

Fear of loss is stronger than desire for gain. Use it ethically — to help, not manipulate.

❌ "At your own risk" (shame)
✓ "You could lose 17 years of Gmail history"

🔍 "Ask Mom" Test — 3 rules

Talk to 3 users before fixing anything. But ask the right way:

✓ Ask about past facts, not future intentions
✓ Ask about their life, not your idea
✓ Look for stories, not opinions

"What was the hardest part last time you ordered?" ≫ "Would you use this?"

⚡ PM + Claude workflow

Screenshot → Claude diagnoses → 3 user interviews → form hypothesis → A/B test → ship. No designer needed at the diagnosis stage.

Insight 3 — 7 Things to Check. A Claude Prompt for Each.

Copy these prompts.
Use them today.

📋
1. Clear value proposition
"Rewrite this headline so a 5th grader instantly knows what they get and why it matters now."
👥
2. Social proof
"Suggest 3 ways to add social proof using the numbers I have: [X users, Y reviews]."
🤝
3. Respectful UX & copy
"Does any text on this screen shame the user? Rewrite to be respectful while keeping conversion intent."
⚡
4. Instant feedback
"What should the user see within 2 seconds of completing this action? Give 3 options."
🚫
5. Remove distractions
"List every element competing with the main CTA. Which 3 can I remove without losing value?"
🪜
6. Break into steps
"Split this flow into steps. Use Zeigarnik — show progress and what's already done."
💎
7. Remind of value at every step
"At each step of this [checkout/onboarding], add a line reminding what they'll get at the end. Under 8 words."
Insight 4 — Unit Economics

AI output ≠ business value

The Surprise

Our AI recommendations were technically perfect — high conversion on popular products.

Zero margin.

AI optimized for engagement. We needed contribution margin.

If you can't calculate the cost of one AI response, you're not ready for production.

3 Questions Before Any AI Feature

1

Which business metric are we moving?

Not clicks. Not engagement. A line on the P&L. Define it before writing the prompt.

2

What does one AI response cost?

Tokens + human review time + prompt maintenance. Hidden costs kill unit economics.

3

Where is "good enough"?

AI doesn't need to be perfect. It needs to be profitable. Set the threshold before launch.

Insight 5 — Real Data Changes Everything

We loaded real store data.
The map broke. That was the win.

We fed the full store dataset directly into the prompt. Real coordinates. Real statuses. Real GMV. The map broke — and that was the game changer.

❌ Without data
Even pin distribution
Fake clusters
Looked ready to ship
✓ With real data
80% activity in 20% pins
Massive density clusters
Layout completely broke
What real data revealed:
✓ Full clustering system needed
✓ Pin hierarchy rethought
✓ Zoom behavior rebuilt
✓ Priority scoring by GMV
✓ No designer imagines this at a desk
The Principle
Data is not content.
Data is the design tool.

Add it on day one. It changes the design, not just the content.

The Takeaway

Engineers are not
the bottleneck.
The right question is.

A PM with real data + AI tools can prototype production-ready products in days. But the question of what to build and why — that's still the human job.
